concise_definition: Baseline runner offense : Strategies as well as general tactics that utilize baseline runner action to create one or more potential scoring opportunities, especially from three-point range.

sections.heading: What is baseline runner action in basketball?
sections.text: Baseline runner action in basketball consists of an offensive player who primarily cuts from one side of the court to the other side by way of the baseline, ultimately to get open.

sections.heading: What is baseline runner offense?
sections.text: Baseline runner offense comprises strategies as well as general tactics that utilize baseline runner action to create one or more potential scoring opportunities, especially from three-point range.

sections.heading: How to understand the player roles and diagrams on this page
sections.text:
In reference to any diagrams below, each of the offensive and defensive players are generally represented by the standardbasketball positions, unless otherwise noted.

Additionally, offensive players are signified by numbers such as 1-5 while defensive players are indicated by the letter X, which is then followed by numbers such as X1-X5.

In essence, 1 and X1 indicate the respective offensive and defensivepoint guards. 2 and X2 signify the respective offensive and defensiveshooting guards.

3 and X3 denote the respective offensive and defensivesmall forwards. 4 and X4 designate the respective offensive and defensivepower forwards.

5 and X5 represent the respective offensive and defensivecenters.

Moreover, in terms of a brief legend for the diagrams:

- Black Dot or Small Circle:The black dot or small circle represents the basketball.

- Solid Arrow:The solid arrow indicates movement or a cut on the basketball court.

- Dotted Arrow:The dotted arrow represents a basketball pass.

- Wavy Arrow:The wavy arrow indicates dribbling.

- T-Shaped Symbol:The T-shaped symbol signifies a screen, also known as a pick.

- Double pipe symbol:The double pipe symbol denotes handoff action.

sections.heading: What is a method to use baseline runner action within 1-4 low offense?
sections.text:
To use baseline runner action within1-4 low offense, a player initially in one corner could cut through via various screens to the opposite wing.

It is also possible to implementhammer actionas a secondary scoring option within the overall 1-4 offensive scheme.

The example diagrams below demonstrate this concept.

sections.heading: Part 1: Baseline runner
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: An example of baseline runner action within 1-4 low offense.]

[Image Alt Text: An example of baseline runner action within 1-4 low offense.]

To begin, 3 runs the baseline from the left side corner towards the right side wing via screens set by 5, 4, and 2. Also, 2 cuts across the lane towards the left side wing after setting their particular screen.

From there, 3 receives the ball and has the opportunity to take the three-point shot if that is open.

sections.heading: Part 2: Hammer
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: An example of hammer action following the baseline runner.]

[Image Alt Text: An example of hammer action following the baseline runner.]

Next, if 3 doesn’t take the possible jump shot, then 3 could begindribblingthe ball towards the baseline via thestep-up screenset by 4.

As that happens, 2 could cut towards the left side corner via the hammer screen set by 5.

Afterwards, 3 could attempt afield goalaround the basket or throw thedrift passwith 2 as the recipient. From there, 2 could take the three-point shot if that is feasible.

Related:Baseline Runner Action – YouTube (shorts)

sections.heading: What is a method to use baseline runner action within zone offense?
sections.text:
To utilize baseline runner action within zone offense, it’s possible to execute a dribble handoff followed by a screen that is set inside of the three-point line near the corner.

The example diagram below showcases this offensive concept.

It should also be noted that this particular baseline runner is implemented against a2-3 zone defense.

[Image Alt Text: An example of baseline runner action within zone offense.]

[Image Alt Text: An example of baseline runner action within zone offense.]

To start, 1 and 3 perform dribble handoff action followed by 3 dribbling the ball back towards the top, which should shift the zone defenders as well.

As that happens, 2 begins the baseline run action from the left corner towards the right side corner. At the same time, 4 cuts down to set an exit screen on the backside defender, shown as X3 on the diagram.

Furthermore, 3 continues dribbling towards the right side wing, previously occupied by 4. Afterwards, assuming the timing is correct, 2 should be momentarily open in the right corner to receive the ball from 3 and take the open three-point shot.

Related:2-3 Zone Offense

sections.heading: What is a method to use baseline runner action within spread offense?
sections.text:
To use baseline runner action within spread offense, aback screencould first be set followed by the player who would run the baseline initially moving to the corner from the adjacent wing.

After that, the baseline runner could cut through to the opposite corner, receive the ball, and shoot if open.

The example diagrams below illustrate this idea in greater detail.

sections.heading: Part 1: Back screen
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: An example of a back screen within spread offense.]

[Image Alt Text: An example of a back screen within spread offense.]

To begin, 1 passes the ball with 3 as the intended target. Following that, 4 cuts to the basket via a back screen set by 5.

If 4 is open, then 4 could receive the ball from 3 and score near the rim. Otherwise, 1 receives the ball again from 3.

Moreover, 5 cuts to the left side low post area while 3 shifts to the right corner and 4 cuts out of the lane towards the right side low post area.

Related:Saint Vincent College Bearcats – Early Offense: Baseline Runner High/Low – FastModel Sports

sections.heading: Part 2: Baseline runner
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: An example of baseline runner action after a back screen happens.]

[Image Alt Text: An example of baseline runner action after a back screen happens.]

Next, 1 dribbles across the court towards the left side wing area. Simultaneously, 2 cuts toward the left side lane line to set screens alongside 4 and 5.

Also at the same time, 3 runs the baseline towards the left side corner using those screens in the process.

3 could receive possession of the ball from 1 and take the three-point shot if that is available.

sections.heading: What is a method to use baseline runner action within continuity offense?
sections.text:
To use baseline runner action withincontinuity offense, a corner player could cut through to the opposite wing via screens set by other players near the low post areas.

Furthermore, one of the low post players could then receive a screen and cut to the high post, leading to additional scoring opportunities.

Afterwards, the players could set up the offense again and run it as a continuity pattern on the other side of the court.

The example diagrams below demonstrate these concepts with more clarity.

sections.heading: Part 1: Baseline runner
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: An example of baseline runner action within continuity offense.]

[Image Alt Text: An example of baseline runner action within continuity offense.]

To begin, 3 receives the ball from 1 and as that happens, 2 runs the baseline towards the left side wing via the screens set by and 5. Afterwards, 2 receives the ball from 3 and has an opportunity to take the three-point shot.

Conversely, if that is not open, then 4 could cut to the high post area via thedown screenset by 1, which also completes thescreen the screeneraction. From there, 4 could receive the ball from 2 and take the mid-range jump shot.

Alternatively though, 5 could duck in to the lane, receive the ball from 4 via thehigh lowaction, and quickly score around the basket.

However, if 4 is not open and 2 retains possession of the ball, then 1 could self replace back to the right slot while 5 fills the right side low post area.

sections.heading: Part 2: Continuity setup
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: An example that showcases the setup for the continuity pattern after the baseline runner action.]

[Image Alt Text: An example that showcases the setup for the continuity pattern after the baseline runner action.]

This next part basically showcases player movement that sets up the continuity pattern of the overall offense.

In essence, 2 dribbles toward the left slot area while 3 cuts through to the left side corner. Furthermore, 4 also cuts down to the left side low post area.

sections.heading: Part 3: Baseline runner continuity
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: An example of the baseline runner action performed on the other side of the floor via the continuity.]

[Image Alt Text: An example of the baseline runner action performed on the other side of the floor via the continuity.]

At this point, the players are occupying spaces that are similar to the first part of the offensive action.

Moving on, 1 receives the ball from 2. As that occurs, 3 runs the baseline via the screens set by 4 and 5, receives the ball from 1, and can take the three-point shot if that is open.

On the other hand, if it is not open, then 4 could cut to the high post area via the down screen set by 2, receive the ball from 3, and take the mid-range shot.

As an alteration, 5 could duck in, receive the ball from 4 via high low action, and score quickly near the rim.

Nevertheless, if 4 is not open and 2 retains possession, then 1 could self-replace back to the left slot while 5 occupies the  left side low post.

sections.heading: Part 4: Continuity setup 2
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: An example of the continuity being set up once again on the other side of the floor.]

[Image Alt Text: An example of the continuity being set up once again on the other side of the floor.]

Once again, the players can prepare for the continuity pattern. Therefore, 3 could dribble up towards the right slot area while 1 cuts through to the right side corner and 4 cuts down to the right side low post area.

From that point, 2 would receive the ball from 3 while 1 runs the baseline towards the left side wing and so on.
